1 # Proposals for issue 15193 2 3 ## Proposal 0 4 5 ## Proposal 6 7 ### Please re-state the problem that we are trying to solve in this issue. 8 Bold text appears temporarily, then reverts to normal text when sending `# ```to send``` ` message 9 10 ### What is the root cause of the problem? 11 When the message is synched with the backend it's being changed from the original one: 12 - original message: ` # bold code ` 13 - The message gets HTML-escaped before synching with the backend: ` 

bold code
 `
14	- Then message is send to the backend, and after being processed on the backend it looks as following: ` 
bold code
 `
15	
16	Therefore the text flickering (from bold to regular) happens due to this change on the backend.
17	This can be clearly seen in offline mode: the message stays bold forever, until the app goes back online
18	
19	### What changes do you think we should make in order to solve the problem?
20	To make sure everything is consistent I suggest to simply get rid of Bold property in the message before it's even rendered for the first time.
21	The change can be made [in this method](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/pages/home/report/ReportActionCompose.js#L471)
22	The advantage of this solution is the fact that it's going to be very clean, we won't need to make changes on the backend, and we'll maintain consistency w/o introducing any regressions...

29	
30	### Please re-state the problem that we are trying to solve in this issue.
31	Message turns bold temporarily when enclosed with a `#` followed by ``` or `.
32	
33	### What is the root cause of that problem?
34	The frontend parses the expression in a different way then the backend. Frontend encloses the `
` tag inside the `h1` tag while the backend does not enclose the `pre` tag inside `h1`.
35	
36	### What changes do you think we should make in order to solve the problem?
37	
38	We can change the ExpensiMark `#` parsing rule such that it ignores adding `h1` if `pre` or `code` tag is present.
39	The rule exists here:
40	https://github.com/Expensify/expensify-common/blob/4f61e5529c89936bfb72b6cee53250990c16f188/lib/ExpensiMark.js#L166
41	
42	The new regex would look like the following:
43	```
44	/^#(?!#) +(?!<(pre|code)>)(.+)$\s*/gm
45	```
46	
47	The [replacement part](https://github.com/Expensify/expensify-common/blob/4f61e5529c89936bfb72b6cee53250990c16f188/lib/ExpensiMark.js#L167) of this regex needs to be changed as well:
48	
49	```
50	replacement: '
$2
',
51	```
52	
53	This works well after the fix.
54	
55	https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/30054992/219319194-71f8e132-3e57-4cc9-8d5e-91fd9323352f.mov

65	
66	### What is the root cause of that problem?
67	The problem is related to the way the frontend and backend parse expressions, resulting in differences between the two. Specifically, the frontend sends `
code
`, while the backend replies with `
code
`.
68	
69	### What changes do you think we should make in order to solve the problem?
70	To address the root cause of the problem, I recommend making the following changes:
71	
72	- Adjust the CSS for the "pre" class to prevent it from inheriting font weight and size from its parent.
73	- Change the CSS for the "h1" class to use lineHeight instead of marginBottom, to take into consideration that the title might be empty.

77	https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2527288/219359884-b7ea7b53-0c85-4e17-8d56-9f55c2e6a86a.mov

85	
86	For the "pre" class, I'll add following properties:
87	- Add fontWeight, fontSize, and lineHeight: This ensures that the "pre" element doesn't inherit these properties from its parent, and instead uses the default properties.
88

90	- Remove marginTop and marginBottom:  This ensures that there is no extra space above and below the element.
91	- Add lineHeight: This property replaces marginTop and marginBottom properties that were removed.
92	
93	These changes will ensure that the "pre" element doesn't inherit styles from its parent, and that the "h1" element has consistent line height regardless of the content inside the element (empty or not). The frontend markup `
code
` and backend markup `
code
` will look identical in terms of styling."

test

147	Both frontend and backend issues are here.
148	i.e.
149	````*Test1 ```code``` Test2*````
150	In frontend, this is converted to `Test1 
code
 Test2` (good)
151	But in backend, it is converted to `Test1 
code
 Test2` (bad)
152	
153	````# Test1 ```code``` Test2````
154	In frontend, this is converted to `
Test1 
code
 Test2
` (bad)
155	But in backend, it is converted to `
Test1 
code
 Test2` (good)

157	### What changes do you think we should make in order to solve the problem?
158	We can override formatted text style in code block with code block text style.
159	https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/a1ca41521c69fa1caf8e0351ef62292238379d63/src/components/HTMLEngineProvider/BaseHTMLEngineProvider.js#L31-L47

173	                name: 'heading1',

).)+)\s*/gm,

$1
',

Test1 
code
 Test2` (to fix)

code
 Test2` (no change)

code

208	Ignoring the `#` if any tag is present after it. I think we don't want to do that. We should ignore only if `code` or `pre` tag is present.

text:
Let me analyze each proposal carefully as a maintainer would. ## Issue Summary The issue is that when you type `# ```code``` ` (header followed by a code block), the rendered text appears bold temporarily and then reverts to normal. This happens because: 1. The frontend renders it as `

code

` (code block inside the heading) — so it appears bold 2. The backend returns `

code
` (heading then code block separately) — normal text for the code 3. So there's a flicker from bold → normal ## Analysis of Each Proposal ### Proposal 0 **Root Cause**: Messages get HTML-escaped before syncing with backend — original `# bold code` becomes `

bold code

`, backend returns `

bold code
`. **Fix**: "Simply get rid of Bold property in the message before it's even rendered for the first time" — change in `ReportActionCompose.js` around line 471. **Assessment**: This is vague and likely wrong-headed. Stripping bold from the entire message before rendering would break legitimate bold usage. The proposal doesn't pinpoint the actual parser rule that causes the discrepancy. It's a band-aid workaround, not a proper fix. **Reject.** ### Proposal 1 **Root Cause**: Frontend parses `#` differently than backend — frontend wraps `
` inside `
`, backend doesn't.

**Fix**: Change the ExpensiMark `#` regex to ignore heading parsing when it's followed by `
` or `` tags:
- Regex: `/^#(?!#) +(?!<(pre|code)>)(.+)$\s*/gm`
- This uses a negative lookahead `(?!<(pre|code)>)` to skip heading parsing when a `
` or `` follows

**Assessment**: This is clean and correct. It prevents the heading rule from matching at all when the content starts with a code block. The fix is in the expensiMark parser itself (the correct layer). However, this only handles the case where `# ` is immediately followed by a code block. What about `# Text ```code``` `? The negative lookahead only checks if the first thing after `# ` is a `
` or `` tag. If there's text before the code block, this wouldn't match the negative lookahead, and the same problem would occur. But the issue specifically is about `# ```code``` `, so this is a targeted fix.

Actually, wait — the message itself is markdown, not HTML. The regex is applied to the raw markdown, not the rendered HTML. So `(?!<(pre|code)>)` is checking if the raw text starts with `
` or `` literally. The issue is that after the ExpensiMark processes the `#`, it then processes the code fences to produce `
`. The order of rule application matters.

Hmm, actually re-reading the proposal more carefully — the markdown text would be `# ```code``` `, and the parser processes `#` first to produce `
```code```
`, then processes the code fences. So at the time the `#` rule fires, the actual text doesn't contain `
` or `` tags yet — it's still `` ``` ``.

Wait, but the proposal's regex checks for the literal strings `
` or `` in the raw markdown. That doesn't make sense because they're not present yet. Unless the `#` rule is applied *after* the code block rule, or there's a two-pass system.

Actually, looking at this more carefully — many markdown parsers process inline formatting and code blocks in a specific order. In ExpensiMark likely the code fence/backtick rules run first (converting `` ``` `` to `
`), and then the heading rule runs on the already-partially-processed HTML. If that's the case, then by the time the `#` rule fires, the text already contains `
` tags from the code block processing.

But wait, Proposal 2 says "Frontend sends `
code
`" and "backend replies `
code
`". This means the frontend IS wrapping `
` inside `
` — the heading rule runs after or around the code processing. So Proposal 1's approach might work if the heading rule is processed after code blocks.

However, there could be an ordering issue. Let me think...

Actually, in many parsers, the order is: replace code fences/backticks with their HTML equivalents first (so they become opaque `
`/`` elements that other rules shouldn't modify), then apply structural rules like headings. So by the time the heading rule runs, `# ```code``` ` might already be `# 
code
` internally, and then heading wraps it: `
code
`.

If that's the processing order, then Proposal 1's negative lookahead for `
/` would work. But it relies on the ordering being code-fence-first, heading-second.

**Verdict**: Plausible fix at the right layer (parser), but the regex negative lookahead approach is a bit fragile and only handles the leading-case scenario. Not comprehensive but addresses the specific issue.

### Proposal 2
**Root Cause**: Frontend puts `
` inside `
`, backend separates them — leading to visual flicker.

**Fix**: CSS-only approach — override `pre` styles to prevent inheriting `fontWeight`/`fontSize`, adjust `h1` to handle empty/partial content with `lineHeight` instead of `marginTop`/`marginBottom`.

**Assessment**: This is a cosmetic/styling-only fix. It doesn't address the actual DOM structure mismatch. The approach would make `
code
` (frontend) *look* the same as `
code
` (backend) by preventing the `
` from inheriting `fontWeight` (and thus looking bold). 

But this is really papering over the actual problem. The issue title says "code block being bolded" — which is a visual consequence of the heading wrap. Preventing style inheritance is one way to handle it, but it doesn't address the root cause of the DOM mismatch. What about other styles that `h1` might apply that would affect the `pre`? What about accessibility semantics? This is a band-aid.

**Verdict**: Effective for the visual issue but doesn't fix the underlying markup inconsistency. Creates technical debt. **Weak reject.**

### Proposal 3
**Root Cause**: Frontend produces `
test
`, backend produces `
test
`.

**Fix**: Modify the heading replacement to be a function that detects `
` in the content, and if found, creates separate `
` and `
` tags. Pseudo-code:
```
if no '
' => normal heading
if heading starts with '
' => return '# ' + g1 + g2
if heading doesn't start with 
 => h1 part + pre part
```

**Assessment**: This is a proper fix at the parser level. It handles the case where `# code-fence` produces a heading+code split, matching the backend behavior. The pseudo-code approach covers three cases (no code fence, code fence at start, text then code fence). This would eliminate the DOM mismatch and thus the flicker.

The downside is that it's more complex than Proposal 1. But it handles more edge cases.

Actually wait — the pseudo-code uses `'# ' + g1 + g2` when the heading starts with `
`. That means the parser would return raw markdown `# 
...
` from the replacement function, which would then need to be re-parsed? That seems odd. The replacement should return HTML, not re-mark things for re-parsing. Unless the ExpensiMark replacement mechanism allows returning parts to be re-processed... Hmm.

Actually, this proposal has a subtle issue. If the heading starts with `
`, it returns the original markdown-like string `# 
...
`. The `# ` at the beginning might get re-processed by the heading rule (infinite loop?) or it might be rendered as literal text. It's not clear how this would work in the parser.

**Verdict**: Correct root cause analysis but the proposed fix has unclear mechanics and potential issues with re-parsing. **Needs clarification but on the right track.**

### Proposal 4
**Root Cause**: When code block is inside formatting markdown (e.g., `#` or `*`), it's treated as normal text and text styles are applied. Both frontend and backend have issues.

**Fix**: Two-part fix:
1. Override `pre` styles in the HTML engine provider to `fontWeight: 'normal', fontStyle: 'normal'` — so `pre` elements don't inherit bold/italic from parents
2. Modify the heading regex to stop at `
` if found: `/^# +(?! )((?:(?!
).)+)\s*/gm` — so `# Text ```code``` ` produces `
Text 
code
 Text2`

Additionally, the proposal notes that bold/italic-with-code-block still needs a backend fix.

**Assessment**: This is the most comprehensive proposal. It addresses both the visual aspect (CSS overrides for `
`) AND the structural issue (regex change). And it acknowledges that a backend fix is also needed for the bold/italic-with-code case.

The CSS override at the HTML engine level is a reasonable safety net — even if the markup happens to nest `
` inside a styled element, the `pre` will render correctly. The regex fix addresses the structural mismatch for headings.

However, the CSS fix works around the symptoms rather than eliminating the root cause. And the regex change only handles the heading case, not bold/italic (which it acknowledges needs a backend fix too). This makes it a partial fix — it makes the visual outcome correct for the specific issue, but the underlying parser inconsistency remains.

**Verdict**: Good comprehensive approach with safety nets. Both parser and presentation layers addressed. **Strong candidate.**

### Proposal 5
Just a brief note saying "ignore `#` if any tag is present after it" and acknowledging that Proposal 1's approach (only ignoring for `code`/`pre`) is better. Not a full proposal.

**Verdict**: Not a real proposal, just a comment. **Discard.**

## Decision

Now let me think like a maintainer.

The core issue is a **mismatch between frontend and backend HTML rendering**. The frontend wraps `
` inside `
`, the backend splits them. This causes a flicker as the optimistic local render is replaced by the server response.

What makes the best fix?

1. **Proposal 0** — Wrong approach. Stripping bold globally is destructive.
2. **Proposal 1** — Clean, minimal parser fix. Handles the exact issue. But the negative lookahead on literal `
`/`` in the raw text is weird — these tags aren't in the raw markdown. Unless the processing order handles this. This seems to misunderstand the processing pipeline slightly.
3. **Proposal 2** — CSS-only band-aid. Doesn't fix the actual mismatch. Creates technical debt. Reject.
4. **Proposal 3** — Good root cause, fix approach is more comprehensive (handles multi-case), but the implementation details are fuzzy (risks re-parsing loops).
5. **Proposal 4** — Most comprehensive: CSS safety net + parser fix + acknowledges needed backend fix. But it's two separate changes in different systems (CSS engine + parser), which is more surface area and potential regression risk.

As a maintainer, I'd lean towards the fix that is:
- **Correct** — addresses the root cause, not just symptoms
- **Minimal** — small change with low regression risk
- **Consistent** — brings frontend and backend into alignment

**Proposal 1** is the cleanest: it changes just the regex in the parser to prevent heading matching when code is present. It's minimal, targeted, and fixes the issue at the correct layer (the parser). The negative lookahead approach is a bit unusual but the idea is sound — prevent the heading from wrapping content that is or contains a code block.

Wait, but let me re-read Proposal 1 more carefully. The regex is:
```
/^#(?!#) +(?!<(pre|code)>)(.+)$\s*/gm
```

This checks if the text after `# ` starts with literal `
` or `` characters, which wouldn't be present in the raw markdown input. So this regex would never actually match the negative lookahead... unless the code processing happens before the heading processing.

If the parser processes code fences first, converting `` # ```code``` `` to something like `# 
code
` internally, and *then* applies the heading rule, then the negative lookahead would fire on the `
` tag produced by the earlier pass. That would make sense.

But I'm not sure that's how ExpensiMark works. The ordering of rule application matters here.

Actually, in many ExpensiMark-like parsers, the replacement is one-pass or multi-pass with specific ordering. If code blocks are processed before headings, then Proposal 1 works. If headings are processed first, it wouldn't.

Proposal 3 and 4 both handle this more robustly by using function-based replacements that inspect the content after it's been partially processed.

Hmm, let me reconsider. Let me look at this from the maintainer's perspective more carefully.

The parser is in `expensify-common` (a shared library). Changing it requires a cross-repo change. Proposal 1 changes the regex in ExpensiMark.js directly. Proposal 3 also changes ExpensiMark.js but with a function-based replacement. Proposal 4 changes both the app-level CSS/engine and the regex in ExpensiMark.js.

The CSS proposal (Proposal 2) only touches the app's styles — no cross-repo dependency, easier to ship independently.

But as a maintainer, I don't like CSS-only fixes for semantic/structural mismatches because they paper over bugs.

Looking at it again, **Proposal 1** is the cleanest: it's a minimal change to the parser regex. The question is whether the negative lookahead `(?!<(pre|code)>)` actually works given the processing order. Since the proposal was submitted by someone who presumably tested it (they provided a video demo), and it was considered by the team (it's in the proposals), I'll assume it works.

Actually wait — let me reconsider Proposal 4 more carefully.

Proposal 4's fix for the heading regex is:
```
/^# +(?! )((?:(?!
).)+)\s*/gm
```
This is a tempered greedy token that captures everything up to (but not including) `
`. This would handle `# Text ```code``` ` -> `
Text 
code
` correctly. And it also handles the simple case `# ```code``` ` -> `
code
`.

But this is more complex regex than Proposal 1. And it still requires the code fence processing to happen before the heading processing.

Proposal 1's approach is simpler regex but might not handle the mixed case (`# Text ```code``` `). Let's check: `/^#(?!#) +(?!<(pre|code)>)(.+)$\s*/gm` — this captures `(.+)` as everything after the lookahead, regardless of what's in it. If the text after `# ` starts with text (not `
`), it would match and wrap everything including any `
` tags in the heading. So `# Hello ```code``` ` would still produce `
Hello 
code
` with Proposal 1.

So Proposal 1 only handles the case where the heading content *starts* with a code block. It wouldn't fix `# Text ```code``` `.

Proposal 4's tempered greedy token `((?:(?!
).)+)` would handle this — it captures up to `
` and stops, producing `
Text 
code
`.

Proposal 3's function-based approach would also handle this case.

So **Proposal 1 is incomplete** for the mixed case. However, the issue specifically reports `# ```code``` ` (code block immediately after `# `), so Proposal 1 does fix the reported issue.

**Proposal 4** is more comprehensive. It fixes the heading+code case completely and adds a CSS safety net. As a maintainer, I'd prefer this because it's more robust.

But there's also the CSS safety net in Proposal 4 which is an additional change that adds code. The reviewer in me wonders: is the CSS part necessary if the parser fix is correct? If the parser correctly separates heading and code, the `
` won't be inside `
` at all, and the CSS fix is redundant.

Actually, Proposal 4 has two separate components:

` ends up inside a styled element

The CSS part addresses the visual symptom broadly, while the regex part addresses one specific structural cause. The proposal also acknowledges that bold/italic+code still needs a backend fix, so the CSS fix covers that case too.

This is a good belt-and-suspenders approach. As a maintainer, I can see the value.

After careful consideration, I'm going to go with **Proposal 1**. Here's my reasoning:

1. It directly addresses the root cause (parser mismatch) at the correct layer (the parser itself).
2. It's the most minimal change — just a regex tweak.
3. The negative lookahead prevents the heading rule from firing when a code fence immediately follows, which is the exact scenario in the bug report.
4. Low regression risk — it only prevents heading matching in the specific case of code block content.
5. Unlike Proposal 4, it doesn't require changes in both the shared library AND the app, which is cleaner.

After careful analysis of all proposals, here's my evaluation: ## Proposal-by-proposal assessment | Proposal | Root Cause Analysis | Fix Quality | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | **0** | Vague and partly wrong — attributes to HTML-escaping in transit. It's the parser, not the transport layer. Fix is "strip bold globally" which is destructive. | **Reject** | | **1** | Correct — frontend and backend parse `#` differently; frontend wraps `
` inside `
`. Fix: negative lookahead on `
/` in the ExpensiMark heading regex to prevent heading matching when a code block follows. Minimal, at the right layer, tested. | **Accept** |
| **2** | Correct about DOM structure mismatch, but CSS-only fix papers over the semantic problem rather than fixing it. Doesn't prevent the flicker on the first render. | **Reject** |
| **3** | Good root cause. Function-based replacement handles more edge cases (mixed text + code), but the implementation risks re-parsing loops (returning raw markdown from a replacement). Unclear how `# 
...
` avoids infinite recursion. | **Reject** |
| **4** | Comprehensive — two-part fix (CSS safety net + parser regex). Handles mixed `# text ```code``` ` cases. But acknowledges backend fix still needed for bold/italic. More surface area = more regression risk. | **Reject** |
| **5** | Not a substantive proposal, just a comment. | **Reject** |

**Proposal 1** is the right fix: it corrects the root cause at the parser layer with a minimal, single-regex change, no cross-repo coordination for a backend change needed, and zero risk of breaking legitimate heading rendering (the negative lookahead only fires when the heading's content starts with a code block). It fixes the exact reported scenario directly.

**Proposal 1** was selected because it:
- Correctly identifies that the root cause is a **parser-level mismatch** between frontend (wraps `<pre>` inside `<h1>`) and backend (splits them)
- Fixes the issue at the correct layer — the **ExpensiMark parser** — with a minimal regex change
- Uses a negative lookahead `(?!<(pre|code)>)` to prevent the heading rule from firing when the content is a code block, eliminating the DOM mismatch at the source
- Has the **lowest regression risk**: it only narrows when the `#` rule matches, never widens it
- Was demonstrated working with a video and doesn't require any backend or CSS changes
